1.englishTranslation: Jan Długosz, The Life of Saint Cunigunde

5.originalText: Reuerendissimo in xpo patri domino Iacobo de Seno dei gratia Cracouien. Ecclesie Episcopo domino meo colendissimo Iohannes Longini Canonicus Cracouien. obedientiam cum famulatu reverentiam et in salute perhenne que omnia vera christus est se feliciter dirigi possit et consumari. Themistocles atheniensis pater reverendissime homo generis nobilitate prestans et operibus supra omnes Grecorum duces marinis celebratus laudibus qui et facillimum virtutum genus ceteris excelluit adeo inflammatus ad gloriam adeo rerum maximarum ambitione honoris cupidus et aliene imitationis emulator extitisse autor plutarchus est scribit ut sub tempore quo apud Marathonem Xerxes ille Persarum rex potentissimus et cum vi potentia pelago vi capiebat et terra a parva et languida grecorum manu callido magis ingenio et arte quam armis et robore Miltiade duce superatus est: iuvenis admodum celebratam tunc Miltiadis gloriam per ora frequenti laude virum tactitus apud se volvens diu rursum amo relati ex barbaris triumphi senem et magnitudine metiens stimulis virtutum agitatus dolore instigari totus quod non sibi potius quam Miltiadi tam celebris triumphi decus fortuna tribuisset nocturnis vigiliis noctes inquietas a se erigere repudiare se consuetus convivis et amicorum familiaritatibus indulsit. Rogantibus vero et admirantibus vite eius mutationem et quid ita eo tempore in publico usque rarius quaerentibus responderet Miltiadis tropheum sibi somnos adimere Marathon siquidem arthynusium et Salamina navalis victorie fertilia nomina tacitis faucibus animum adolescentis fervent et glorie cupidum ad similia edenda accendebat opera adeo ut circum quietis dulcedinem vonenter ei in sequeset. Quorsum hec ex gestis gentilium tam longe repetita paria propior quisque lector intelligat me maiori indignatione super neglectu beneficiorum dei polonorum generi per virginem illustrissimam Kunegundim polonie ducissam polonis prestitorum quam Themistocles super aliene fortune eventu ambicionis livore comoveri. Nec siquidem ego sepe nio mecum reputans vehementer excitari in admiracionem et indignationem quandam verti soleo vehe mentem quidem et iustam quoniam ad consequendam gloriam triumphales Miltiadis claritates sit Themistocles per femina tam celebre tam excellente tam lecta que maximus laboribus sudoribusque suis polonis multa beneficia tribuisset tantum abfuisse ut paria digna suis virtutibus et meritis apud illos reportaret ut quibus saluti prosperitati et fulcementi fuit est et erit et a quibus precipuis laudibus ornada et efferenda fuerat ab his illius nomen excellencia et canonisatio negligatur. Dolui ego non tam ste huius dignis que cum celis colis beatitudinie trinitatem evo sempiterno fruuntur visione sed polonorum forte qui divina beneficia parum pendentes memoriam suam quamvis miro miraculorum splendore choruscant ducerit in prope iam annis ducentis in terre visceribus delitescere. Et Themistocles quidem vane et periture et quod viciosius reputo proprie et private glorie angebatur cura...
5.englishTranslation: To the most reverend father in Christ, Lord Jacobus de Seno, by the grace of God Bishop of the Church of Krakow, my most worshipful lord, Jan Długosz (Johannes Longini), Canon of Krakow, [offers] obedience, service, and reverence, wishing eternal health in Christ, praying that all may be happily directed and brought to completion. The Athenian Themistocles, most reverend father, a man outstanding in nobility of race and celebrated for his maritime deeds above all Greek leaders, who excelled others in a very easy kind of virtue, was so inflamed for glory and so eager for honor in the ambition of great matters, being a rival in the imitation of another, as the author Plutarch writes, that during the time when the most powerful Xerxes, King of the Persians, was seizing sea and land by force and was overcome by a small and weak Greek force through cleverness rather than strength under Miltiades' leadership: the young Themistocles, silently pondering the then-celebrated glory of Miltiades and measuring the old triumph over the barbarians by its magnitude, was driven by the spurs of virtue to feel pain that fortune had granted the honor of such a famous triumph to Miltiades rather than to himself. Shunning his usual nightly rest, he withdrew from social feasts and friendships. When asked in wonder about this change, he replied that Miltiades' trophy robbed him of sleep. Indeed, the naval victories at Marathon, Artemisium, and Salamis stirred the youth's mind and kindled his desire for glory to perform similar works, so much so that he willingly abandoned rest. To what purpose these ancient deeds are recounted, let the reader understand: that I am moved by a greater indignation over the neglect of God's blessings to the Poles through the most illustrious virgin Kunegunda [Saint Kinga], Duchess of Poland, than Themistocles was by the success of another. Reflecting on this, I am often moved to a just indignation, for while Themistocles sought Miltiades' triumphal glory, here is a woman so famous and excellent who bestowed many benefits on the Poles through great toil, yet her name and canonization are neglected by those for whom she was and remains a support. I grieve not for her, who enjoys the eternal vision of the Trinity, but for the Poles who undervalue divine favors and allow her memory, though bright with miracles, to lie hidden in the bowels of the earth for nearly two hundred years. While Themistocles was distressed by a concern for vain, private glory...

6.originalText: Ego pro neglectu divine ac publice disturbor frequenti gemitione que sanctum instat et presentis polonice nacionis homines et in eos presertim quibus nec res neque propria debeat esse cura stomachor et erubesco pro illorum segnicie et ignavitudine qui in virginis prefate canonisacionis decus fructus ingentes polonico regno et glorie affuturos exigua hactenus visi sunt cura impendisse Qui beneficia quidem multiplicia sancte prompte et feliciter deposcendo suscipimus auctorem vero beneficiorum honoribus condignis recognoscere et honorare negligimus Cum itaque pridem heu evum pene nre gesta vitas et mores relegendas i vita gloriose et beate femine kunegundis polonie ducisse virginis celeberrime ac pene divine tradidit michi mansisset indigno melius ve ferebat vitam illius apud polonos in quos mirifica contulit conferet et divina visitatione collativa est beneficia perire iam sese nascitur suum sequuta nonisi per functione et abbasse descripta fuisse ac m ante constitisset mecum alias lras alium qe stilu ea vertere tua primitas festinum ortabat ad qua iura illa potissimum mittet. Itaque genus illius vita religione mores et prodigia cetera que ad illa ptinet in hoc opus convectu congegi undique atte conectens que ad libellū pitis seriem conducere quolibet videantur Tue quoque primitati potissimum hunc libellum vite eius dedicaui quam pridem comptum habebat te inter maximas occupaciones tuas hom divinor et singula summum sere admiracione delectai Cuius dum viveret i primis in suos i familia in ecclesiasticos i religiosos vios in omne denique polonum genus ertabat eius singlaris dilecto et hilaritas a qua omnis puritas et omni ututi gra in singulos manauit status Karus noticia vite eius te et ceteros sacri ordinis vios celsos in ecclia dei obstinctes migratui i felicitatem et amore hui femine et deinde i canonisacionis ardore pertraxit non casuale insup utilitate et si non ad eloquenciam in qua me semp rudum fuisse presatus sum ad bene vivendum tamen in quo vno excelle cuilibet necessarium est pro consequenda vita eterna affertur Que ut plurimum suis virtutibus sua sanctitate et exemplo polonis universis et eorum nacioni prestitit Ita ab illis st honore precipuo habenda colenda et celebranda est et singlaribus ac gloriosis extollanda preconijs In nulla em femina et nra et paulo superiori etatis tot optimas virtutes tot illustres artes et actiones tot preclara facinora conclusse accepimus quot ista ipsa kunegundi que ab ipsis incunabulis ymo ab ipo ortu mirificis divisque actibg floruerat ad excellentissima sanctitatem virginum provectam in corpore erecta et spiritu facilem dum habens ad solitudines curas edendas nisi iam a deo celi statuā Cui summam felicitatem et laude excellentissimam caritas iugnitatis qua in coniugio et in principatu et per omne vite typus servabat tunsit mentem gerens in omni etate sanctam et spontanea animam iustum a trenis prorsus sequestratum et congluinatum superis pectus & religioni se sancte deditum et naturali honestate generosum In comprimendis voluptatum illecebris celestes emulata Virgo aut prefata et si proforiocis suis ac temporibus nondum iustum et emeritum multiplicatione consequuta sit canonisacionis decus nondum ubi templa statuta sunt vel sacre edes nondum illata thuris dignus honor utpote eam in divoru numerum tu facis in diem hac a summopontifice nec petita est nec obtenta Quam vicarius Christi succedens potestate illam in beato apostolo petro a domino accepit canonisandum dignos indignos excludendum
6.englishTranslation: I am disturbed by frequent groaning on account of the neglect of both divine and public duty which weighs upon the holy, and I am annoyed at the men of the present Polish nation, and especially those who should have no concern for their own affairs. I blush for their sloth and ignorance, for they have thus far seemed to bestow little care upon the great fruits of honor for the Polish kingdom and glory that would come from the canonization of the aforementioned virgin. We indeed receive multiple benefits by asking holily, promptly, and happily, yet we neglect to recognize and honor the author of the benefits with worthy honors. Since, therefore, long ago (alas!) the deeds, life, and manners of almost our own time, to be read again in the life of the glorious and blessed woman Kunegunda, the most famous and almost divine virgin duchess of Poland, were handed over to me, unworthy as I am, lest her life—in which she bestowed wonderful things upon the Poles and to whom divine benefits were granted by a collaborative visitation—should perish now that it has been brought forth, having been only partially described by an abbess; and since it had previously been agreed with me to translate it into another style with other letters, your Excellence urged me to be quick, to which end it will most especially send those rights. Therefore, I have collected her lineage, life, religion, manners, and other miracles pertaining to her into this compiled work, connecting them from all sides, which seem in any way to lead to the sequence of this present little book. To your Excellence also I have especially dedicated this little book of her life, which I have long held ready, delighting you among your greatest occupations, a man of divine things, with the highest admiration for each thing. While she lived, she excelled in her singular love and cheerfulness toward her own, her family, the clergy, and religious men, and finally toward the whole Polish race, from which all purity and the grace of every virtue flowed into every rank. The dear knowledge of her life has drawn you and other men of the sacred order, eminent in the Church of God, toward her joyful passing and to love for this woman, and then into the fire of canonization, providing no accidental utility; and even if it does not aim for eloquence, in which I have always confessed my deficiency, yet it is offered for living well, in which alone it is necessary for anyone to excel to obtain eternal life. For she has, for the most part, by her virtues, her holiness, and example, served all Poles and their nation. Thus she is to be held in special honor, worshipped and celebrated by them, and extolled with singular and glorious praises. For in no woman, neither in our age nor a little earlier, have we heard of so many excellent virtues, so many illustrious arts and actions, so many noble deeds concluded, as in this very Kunegunda, who from her very cradle, nay from her very birth, flourished in wonderful and divine acts, brought to the most excellent holiness of virgins, upright in body and having a spirit ready for solitude, as if she were already a statue of heaven from God. Whose highest happiness and most excellent praise the charity of her dignity—which she preserved in marriage, in her principality, and through every model of life—struck into her mind, bearing a holy soul in every age and a spontaneous mind, entirely sequestered from earthly things and a heart joined to the heavens, devoted to holy religion and generous with natural honesty. In suppressing the allurements of pleasures, emulating the celestial ones, the aforesaid Virgin, even if she has not yet obtained the just and well-deserved honor of canonization through the passage of time and the increase of her supporters—so that temples are not yet decreed nor sacred buildings, nor the worthy honor of incense yet offered, as you strive for day by day among the number of the saints—this has not been requested from the supreme pontiff nor obtained. Which power the Vicar of Christ, succeeding in authority, received from the Lord in the blessed Apostle Peter, to canonize the worthy and exclude the unworthy.
